speaker: interviewer
text: September 17th, 1996. The interviewee is General Felix Sparks, the interviewer Toni [INAUDIBLE] Binstock in Lakewood, Colorado, United States of America, and it is being done in English. I think at the end of the last tape, we were talking about that you did not choose to participate in the Nuremberg Trials and you said you were back home and you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was district attorney in western Colorado at that time and I couldn't really have added anything to the Nuremberg Trials and I didn't want to be cross-examined about the killing of those guards. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the interesting things is there have been a number of books written about Dachau-- I have three or four up here in my library now-- and some of them are so outlandish that it's incredible. I have one book says that we killed something like, I think, 545 German soldiers in Dachau. Well, there wasn't half that many there when we got there in the first place. The actual body count done by the inspector general I believe was 34. And some of those were not killed by American soldiers. Some were killed by these camp trustees, I call them, the inmates. There were of course armament everywhere. Rifles-- the Germans dropped their rifles and the inmates killed probably four or five of the-- the 34. Because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --until we got them stopped, they were going to kill every German they could find. And they probably killed maybe four or five, half-- I don't know how many-- half a dozen of the 34 who were killed. But the inspector general-- if I-- in the report that he made was very meticulous. He even counted the bullet holes in the wall where my machine gun had killed those 11. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The number he puts us higher than. 11 but what happened was the-- the-- the prisoners themselves caught some Germans and took them to that place and killed them there. But my men actually-- my machine gunner-- actually killed about 11. But the numbers vary according to who wrote the book. The highest number I've seen is in a book I have here, it's I think 545. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, that's far-fetched from 34. But other books also have equally high numbers of-- of German soldiers who were killed there. The true numbers is 34. I don't care if there were 500. If we had killed 500, I would say so. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's no reason for me to worry about how many were killed there. I-- but I was-- I was there. And why they come-- I think a lot of these authors in order to embellish their book have to exaggerate their numbers. I find that quite common among authors who weren't there who are writing about historic events, they want to embellish their story. And I'm sure that's the case. Some of these books are more or less factual, but none of them are accurate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But anyway, I was always intrigued by all these books that said how many we had killed. And I knew it wasn't the case. But after-- immediately after the armistice was declared, which was about May 8, I guess officially it was, right-- a few days after Dachau, we started-- the American-- the Allied forces started rounding up all the SS officers because many were wanted for known atrocities-- the Malmedy massacre during the Battle of the Bulge where American soldiers were lined up and killed. We had the names of some of those officers from witnesses, SS officers. You never found the regular German Army doing things like this. It was always the SS. And they were wanted for a great number of atrocities, so we rounded up all we could find and there were brought to Dachau. And they were kept there in a special-- a special confinement area was constructed. And all were examined then by Allied examiners to determine which ones were responsible for vaarious things. Most are them were turned loose. But those that we could identify were tried and charged and-- and executed as a result of the Nuremberg Trials. But I was always intrigued by all these stories on what-- what happened at Dachau. So in 1988, through a friend, I arranged for an interview for a German SS captain in Germany. And my wife and I and another couple, the four of us, went to Germany and we met this SS officer in Trier, Germany. He could speak pretty good English.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got to spend about a full day with him going over the war and how he felt about it. And I asked him about the-- the story that we had lined up all those people and killed everybody at that wall. He said, well, no, he said, a friend of mine was a lieutenant in that group and he wasn't hurt. So I-- but he said he was there and he witnessed it. And he said an American officer stopped it. Well, that was me. And anyway, I had a long interview with this German officer, former officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was asked then to see if he was any way repentant, and he was very straightforward. He-- but I could tell from our several hours we spent together in a little lodge up in the mountains out of Trier that he wasn't a damn bit repentant. [LAUGHING] It was apparent to me that the only thing he was sorry about was that they got beat. But it was an interesting experience, [INAUDIBLE]. I have received-- I have received some letters from some SS officers since who participated in various battles that we were in. In any event, not many of the SS people were repentant about anything. It was a special group of people and they were tough soldiers. They were fanatical. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I don't think many people realize that we rounded them all up and incarcerated them and grilled them all. And practically all of them are turned loose eventually, but they were kept prisoners for some period of time there right out-- near Dachau. So the-- some of the post war things that I found out are quite interesting, but particularly this file that I have here where the inspector general-- I didn't realize that he'd counted the bullet holes in that wall and he has each one marked where those 11 that I know of where killed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's-- I have studied, I've read everything I could find on it. Some of the books are in foreign tongue that I can't read. They've been given to me by-- another thing that I did not realize was the-- the large-- to the-- to the extent to which the French cooperated with the Germans in concentration camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's been downplayed. But there were concentration camps in France that were manned by Frenchmen. All the-- the overseers were Gestapo. But the French-- the Vichy government cooperated fully in rounding up the Jews in France. And of course, the French today are still after some of the-- I mean, you got a trial of various people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have had an opportunity to talk to a great number of people. I have traveled in various states-- Texas, Oklahoma, Wyoming, Utah, California-- and spoken to various groups. One thing I found out last year-- I was in San Antonio, Texas-- I met a professor, an American professor, who was teaching at Emory University in Atlanta. And he said, general, I bet-- he said, I bet you don't know that there was one country that refused to cooperate with Hitler. And I said, no, I never heard of that. Well, it turned out this professor was from Bulgaria. Now, Bulgaria was an Axis partner, all of Balkan states were. Czechoslovakia was forced it, but Hungary and Romania and-- and Bulgaria were all full partners with Hitler. And he said that Hitler issued an order to the king of Bulgaria [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that 25,000 Jews-- they always set a quota, I found out, in Italy and wherever-- that they were to send into to Germany for extermination. And the Bulgarian king refused to do it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we have a parliament Bulgaria and the parliament refused. So the king was ordered to go to Berlin. He went, came back, and died very mysteriously several days later. The Bulgarians say he was poisoned somehow or other. But then Hitler made a-- he lowered the demand from 25,000 to 20,000 and the Bulgarians still refused. And he didn't-- Hitler didn't do anything about it. So Bulgaria was apparently the only country that refused to-- to round up any Jews. And I found out in Italy, they got a quota the same way, but Italians, while they sent quite a few Jews, they didn't round them up the way Hitler ordered them to. They would always make an excuse they didn't have a transport for-- they use the word transport over there for the trains, you know. And the Italians dragged their feet a lot. And so not many Jews were taken out of Italy. Mussolini wasn't much in favor of taking the Jews out for extermination.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you learned a lot about this after the war that you didn't know before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Well, I didn't know anything about concentration camps before the war. Only after the war on various travels that I've made to countries of Europe and meeting with people who were in those countries or prisoners or like this Bulgarian professor who migrated to the United States, was now a professor in-- at Emory University in Atlanta.

speaker: interviewer
text: You go all over the country, then, speaking, it sounds like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are invited to talk about the Holocaust in that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. High schools and-- and on the military reservations. The military has been active. I've been-- spoken in three different military reservations-- California, Colorado, and Texas-- the military posts. They have a Holocaust memorial at the posts and I've been to three-- four. One, two, three-- one in Utah. Utah, California, Colorado, and Texas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then a number of high schools here and in Wyoming. Do you speak to a particular group like the Anti-Defamation League group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's usually a-- it's usually they have an assembly at these schools and I'm-- sometimes I do it two or three times with different groups of students, different classes. But I'm getting a little old for that anymore. I'm-- I'm going to have to cut it down. My health hasn't been good. But I have learned a tremendous lot about mankind since-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I suppose you can't believe it until you see it. That's-- it's difficult to describe. Because most people say, it couldn't have happened. Well, it did happen. And I might have been among the doubters, I don't know, if I hadn't seen it with my own eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your experiences there affect your religious belief in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I think one thing that I learned and-- I've had some contacts with Muslims. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We live in a difficult world as far as religion is concerned. There are a great number of religions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so immediately, I was surprised to find out that the Muslim religion is the world's largest. I didn't realize that Indonesia and some of those countries are Muslim. But it's the largest-- world's largest religion. So they believe in that religion as devoutly as we might in the Jewish or the Protestant religion. So it's-- it's hard to condemn people. And you've got two or three different prominent religions in India-- the Hindu and-- and they've got different groups over there killing each other. I suppose when you look at the history of the world, religion has been responsible for more deaths than any other single factor in the history of this world. The Crusades and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and all professed to be doing the right thing, but they had no hesitancy of killing or condemning somebody who's not of their religion. To me, that is a form of insanity. And the Catholics and Protestants even in Ireland, it's-- I suppose that something will go on to the end of time. I don't know. But I see no signs that the world is getting much more progressive in the-- in the way of religious tolerance or racial tolerance. Yeah, we're making-- we're getting-- we're making some improvements, but we have a long way to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience in Germany in talking to this SS officer, did that change your feeling or what are your feelings about the German people of your generation or the younger generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I found a lot of Germans were very contrite about the whole Holocaust experience. I remember when-- right after we took Munich, some German I assume had written on one of the public buildings downtown there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'I am ashamed to be a German.' [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And Bavaria-- the Bavarian people were never really hip on Hitler. That at one time was an independent kingdom, of course, and a lot of-- Hitler had a lot of opposition in Bavaria and the center of it was Munich. So you find a lot of-- even today a lot of contrite people, but on the other hand, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] many who are not. I was interested-- we met with the City Council of Dachau here in 1989-- '93, when I was over there. We had a chance to meet with the Bavarian-- or the City Council of Dachau. Now, Dachau is a fairly sizable city, maybe 30,000, 35,000 people, something like that. And at one time, it was quite an arts center, cultural center in Germany back-- way back in the medieval period. It's-- it's a very old city. And they deeply resent the fact that the concentration camp bears the name of their city-- very resentful of that fact. They said, we don't know why we get blamed for Dachau, we had nothing to do with it. They just-- it was an old military installation. I think it was a munitions factory during World War II, that area with Dachau, and the Germans put that concentration camp there. And the present citizens of Dachau are very resentful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's an interesting sidelight. After we took the camp, the citizens of Dachau said, well, we don't know anything about what was going on in there. Well, it's true. They weren't allowed in there. But there were numerous German soldiers who were partying in Dachau all the time, getting drunk, so they would have had some knowledge. And they saw thousands of people going in there and none coming out. But it's true they had no real personal knowledge.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we rounded up all the citizens we could find in Dachau and made them go through Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we made them help bury the bodies. You see, we had so many thousand bodies not only in Dachau, but on the outskirts. Just about two or three weeks before the end of the war, Heinrich Himmler, the head of the Gestapo and the SS, one of the cruelest men that ever lived, sent out an order-- I have a copy of it somewhere-- but it says-- and it was directed to all the commandants of the concentration camps. And the order said, you-- you're not to allow any of the inmates to fall into the hands of Allied troops.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how could they help it? But they were frantic, so they were moving people around like crazy. That's how that train came in from Buchenwald. The Buchenwald commander-- Buchenwald was overrun by Patton's 3rd Army before we took Dachau, about a week or so before. So the commandant of Buchenwald moved a bunch of people, tried to get rid of a bunch. But how could they get rid of 1,000 people? They couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau, the commandant went crazy and he started marching-- we don't know how many thousand, but anywhere from 3,000 to 5,000 that were there-- he started marching them towards Austria. And most of them died. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were policing up bodies for miles around and it turned out we had something like 9,000 dead bodies. So how-- how-- how do you handle that many dead? You can't. But we made the citizens of Dachau help with the burial. And we just took bulldozers and dug deep trenches and tried to identify people with numbers or whatever we could, any way to identify them-- many you could not identify-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and buried them in mass burials. But the-- the people of Dachau was made to witness and to help out with that, you see. What was their reaction to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, many of them were cried and saying they-- well, they didn't know-- they weren't part of it, they didn't know anything about it, which is to a great extent true. The trouble with-- when you get an absolute dictatorship that takes over a country and they take over the police, the ordinary citizen becomes relatively helpless, they're caught. There's not much they could do. And if they did do anything, if they were apprehended then, they were put in a concentration camp or executed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the system gets so vicious that the average citizen can do nothing about it, they're really trapped. And really not much they can do. They can't vote, they couldn't do anything. They just become part of the system.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is true of any absolute dictatorship, the people become helpless. But they allow it to happen in the first instance, that's-- but again, they're rather powerless because suddenly you create a-- Hitler was elected-- well, he wasn't really elected. Germany had free elections and Hitler was not elected. He came out second or-- and the von Hindenberg party won the election with [INAUDIBLE] von Hindenberg was the president of Germany. But the chancellor is the guy who runs it. So Hitler failed in the general election. But the Brown Shirts then got active and they pressured everybody and von Hindenberg appointed Hitler as the chancellor. So he was never elected as the chancellor. And the minute that was done, it was all over. So it was not a free election.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you told-- you've talked to groups-- have you told your children and your grandchildren about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you ever been interviewed by any their oral history projects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I say oral history. There have been-- I-- I appeared in several television films. There was a British crew came here about three years ago from London. They put out a film called It was shown on the Discovery Channel. I was one of the people interviewed in-- for that. [DRINKS] Then a Japanese crew came in about two or three years ago also. [COUGHS] They were trying to-- there was a Japanese battalion that claimed they liberated Dachau, an artillery battalion. Well, in the first place, artillery battalions don't go around liberating anything, they follow up. But they claimed to have liberated Dachau and they claimed that they were told that they couldn't tell anybody that they had- oh, it appeared in the newspapers on the west coast. They had a big reunion in San Francisco. And one of these guys, this Japanese soldier, said-- they were American soldiers, but they were of Japanese ancestry. Well, he met with Cardinal Niemoller-- and Niemoller wasn't even there-- and how they rescued all these people. They weren't there at all. It's true they were in another corps, which was off on the right that was headed towards Austria. What they ran into was this group that was being marched out of Dachau, see. Anyway, they-- the crew was here to try to establish and I disabused them of that in a hurry, I think. But they put out a film on it. They sent me a copy of it, but I-- it won't fit in my thing in here. But they gave me this watch. [LAUGHING] It's a Japanese watch I've never heard of, but it's been running good ever since anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did that in the backyard out here in the summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back at what you experienced and witnessed, is there anything that you would like to say to future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing I can tell future generations is to try to eliminate hatred and prejudice and intolerance for various groups and various races and various religions. Let everybody live their own life, that's my message. And respect the religion or other race. If I don't-- I don't agree with the Muslim religion or the Buddhist, either, but I respect they believe in it and they may be right. None of us know, that's the problem. We-- we're convinced that we're Jewish or Christian, that we have the only religion-- the only true religion. Well, other people feel the same way about their religion. So I am not going to criticize them because they don't feel the same way that I do. And that's the message I try to get across in the speeches that I make. Race, religion, or intolerance has no place in this world and we-- I say we've made a lot of strides, but we're far from perfect in that field and probably never will be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you speak to Holocaust memorials and where all have you been and have you been honored?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I spoke at the dedication of the Holocaust Museum, I guess it was last-- last year. I was one of the four speakers who spoke at that-- they had a tremendous event there. That is a tremendous institution, that Holocaust Memorial Museum. I was amazed at how-- what a tremendous amount of work and money that has gone to constructing that museum. It's right in downtown Washington and it's run by the National Parks Service. And if you want to see that, it takes a good three or four hours. You've got to last-- I would say not less than three hours to see all the exhibits that they have. If anybody who says it didn't happen goes through that museum, I don't know how they can say that all that was fabricated. You know, your mind has got to go-- take a crazy turn to believe that it never happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know. But I talked to one of the museum people last week. They want this book that I have here, this record, which I will send to them. And he told me that they're still getting, oh, well over 1,000 people a day every day through that museum. It's run by the National Parks Service and it's sustained by-- of course, by the federal government. But they do-- I think they charge admission. Yeah, they do charge admission there. I think we'll stop this tape and start another.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know of anything else we can cover.
